answers.answer: Use WebSocket only, extending the existing WS infrastructure. No SSE infrastructure exists in the codebase — implementing SSE would require building chunked HTTP responses, text/event-stream content type, and a separate connection registry from scratch (~150+ lines). The existing WS infrastructure is complete and proven: RFC 6455 helpers (ws_send_text, ws_read_frame, ws_accept_key), WS_CONNECTIONS registry keyed by entity ID, ws_broadcast() fan-out pattern, and _next_ws_seq() for message ordering. The price stream at /ws/markets/{id}/prices demonstrates the full lifecycle (upgrade → register → snapshot → broadcast loop → unregister) that can be replicated for new streams.
evidence: No matches found for SSE, EventSource, text/event-stream anywhere in codebase
answers.uncertainty: None significant. The task title mentions 'websocket/SSE' but the codebase is WS-only and WS covers all requirements.

answers.answer: Use the global _WS_SEQ counter for WS transport-level message ordering on all new streams (consistent with existing price stream). Preserve the per-market event journal seq in the event payload for event-level ordering and integrity. These serve different purposes: _WS_SEQ is for client-side WS gap detection across any stream, while per-market seq is for event journal hash-chain integrity and backfill via REST. For risk streams (per-account), the global _WS_SEQ is sufficient since risk updates don't have a journal with hash chains — they're projections of current state.
answers.uncertainty: Minor: clients subscribing to multiple streams will see interleaved global seq values with gaps, but this is already the case with the price stream and is the expected WS behavior.

answers.answer: No backfill-on-connect. Follow the existing price stream pattern: send an initial snapshot on connect, then push live events going forward. The REST endpoint GET /v1/markets/{id}/events already supports fromSeq parameter with pagination (fromSeq inclusive, limit up to 100, nextFromSeq for continuation). Clients that reconnect can use the REST endpoint to fill gaps between their last-seen per-market seq and current head, then resume the WS stream. This keeps WS handler complexity low and avoids duplicating the pagination/replay logic already in the REST layer.
evidence: backend/server.py:4928-4945 (existing price stream sends snapshot on connect, no backfill)
backend/server.py:2530-2531 (REST fromSeq and limit query parameters)
backend/server.py:2554-2569 (REST response with pagination.nextFromSeq for continuation)
frontend/src/lib/query/hooks.ts:216-231 (frontend already invalidates queries on reconnect)
answers.uncertainty: If high-frequency event markets create noticeable gaps on reconnect, backfill-on-connect could be added later as an enhancement. The REST fallback is functional but adds a round-trip.

answers.answer: Separate endpoints: /ws/markets/{id}/events for market event streams and /ws/accounts/{id}/risk for account risk streams. Keep the existing /ws/markets/{id}/prices unchanged. Rationale: (1) Different data shapes — prices are marginals snapshots, events are journal entries with hash chains, risk is account state with LMSR details. (2) Different update frequencies — prices update on every probability edit, events on every command completion, risk on orders affecting that account. (3) Different scoping — prices and events are per-market, risk is per-account. (4) Follows REST route topology: /v1/markets/{id}/events and /v1/accounts/{id}/risk already exist as separate endpoints. Multiplexing would mix concerns and complicate the message schema.
evidence: backend/server.py:285 (WS_CONNECTIONS keyed by market_id — new registries needed for other entity types)
answers.uncertainty: Two new WS connections per client (if they need all three streams) increases connection count. For a prediction market with moderate users this is fine. Could add multiplexing later if connection limits become an issue.

answers.answer: Broadcast on every account state mutation after sync_account_risk_state() and sync_account_exposure_state() complete. This is not excessively noisy because: (1) Mutations only happen on actual order acceptance — not on background calculations. (2) Each mutation is already scoped to a specific account. (3) The event-sourced model means every state transition is meaningful and clients expect completeness. Broadcast should also fire after settlement (settle_market_account_risk, settle_market_account_exposure) during market resolution, since these cause significant risk state changes.
evidence: backend/server.py:3244 (existing price broadcast fires on every probability edit, same philosophy)
answers.uncertainty: If an account trades heavily (many EventTrades in rapid succession), the risk stream could be chatty. A debounce or batching mechanism could be added later if needed, but start simple.

answers.answer: The event journal stream is sufficient for 'execution streams.' CommandAccepted events for EventTrade orders already carry complete trade details in the payload: trade.side, trade.size, trade.price, trade.notional, trade.marketId, trade.outcomeId, plus effects (marginalDelta, assetDelta) and pricing (cost, fee, unitPrice). A separate per-account order-fill stream would be redundant — clients can filter the market event stream for events matching their account's commandIds, or the event payload could include accountId. No third stream type is needed.
evidence: backend/server.py:4107-4125 (EventTrade CommandAccepted payload with full trade field)
backend/server.py:3847-3851 (ProbabilityEdit CommandAccepted with effects and pricing)
backend/server.py:3629-3641 (event envelope includes commandId for correlation)
answers.uncertainty: If per-account execution filtering is needed (only see your own trades), the event stream is per-market not per-account. Clients would need to correlate commandIds. This is acceptable since the frontend already knows which commands it submitted.

assumption_checks.impact: No SSE implementation needed. Extend existing WS infrastructure with proven helpers, registry, and broadcast pattern. Saves ~150 lines of new transport code.
Price stream (/ws/markets/{id}/prices) stays as-is. New event and risk streams are additive — no refactoring of existing WS handler or frontend consumers needed.

Event streams scoped per-market (matching MARKET_EVENT_SEQUENCES). Risk streams scoped per-account (matching ACCOUNT_RISK/ACCOUNT_EXPOSURE). Connection registries follow same pattern: WS_EVENT_CONNECTIONS[market_id] and WS_RISK_CONNECTIONS[account_id].
Global _WS_SEQ used for transport ordering on all streams. Per-market event seq preserved in event payload for journal integrity. No new atomic counters needed.
Every mutation broadcasts. Mutations are already scoped (only fire on order acceptance and settlement). Not excessively noisy. Can add debouncing later if needed.

Frontend needs: new WS message types (MarketEventMessage, AccountRiskMessage), new useMarketEventSubscription and useAccountRiskSubscription hooks following existing useMarketPriceSubscription pattern, TanStack Query cache integration. queryKeys for accountRisk and accountExposure already exist in hooks.ts.
Initial snapshot + live updates, no backfill-on-connect. REST fromSeq endpoint handles gap recovery. Keeps WS handler simple. Matches existing price stream pattern.

decomposition_assessment.decision_reason: All 6 answers have confidence >= 0.80. All 7 assumptions verified as CORRECT with confidence >= 0.80. No research gaps remain. The implementation follows well-established patterns in the codebase (WS lifecycle, broadcast, registry) with clear mutation points for broadcast hooks. Changes span server.py (new routes + registries + broadcast hooks) and frontend (types + hooks + cache integration) — coordinated but not complex.
